smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
hi Edward, just went through your site, not sure how H2Ocean isn't already on my client list haha.

that's not really why I'm here though. I do Meta ads for ecom brands, and rather than pitch you some BS, I just went ahead and built you a few.

built three ad mockups from H2Ocean's actual products, plus a word-class brand playbook I put together from your site and reviews. it's finished and ready to go on my end.

want me to send it your way?

- Kyle

Kyle 

 Sure send me what you are presenting for Meta Ads, we are interested in the creative content.
 

 Thanks
 Eddie

here you go Edward - 2 ad mockups built from H2Ocean's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
Just set us up for tomorrow. 

 Thanks
 Eddie

copy.tldr: H2Ocean is a 20-year-old natural sea salt mouthwash brand with strong healing claims but no ads running and no visible reviews, so this call is about starting from zero with their Arctic Ocean Mint as the hero product.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A natural oral care brand based in Stuart, Florida, selling a patented sea salt mouthwash. They have been around for over 20 years and say they have over a million satisfied customers. This oral care line looks like an offshoot of a larger H2Ocean brand.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Two products, both the same Healing Rinse mouthwash in different flavors: Arctic Ocean Mint and Fresh Lemon Ice. Each comes in 8oz, 16oz, and 64oz sizes. Made with Red Sea salt, xylitol, and an enzyme called lysozyme. No alcohol, no fluoride, no artificial stuff.
deep_dive.positioning: A premium, natural, non-burning alternative to harsh alcohol mouthwashes. The angle is healing and recovery: post dental work, oral piercings, canker sores, sore throats, dry mouth. Called the original patented sea salt rinse.
deep_dive.who_buys: Health-conscious adults who need gentle relief. People recovering from dental procedures, people with new piercings, canker sore sufferers, and families who want a natural rinse safe for all ages.
customer_voice: This is great for post-dental work and for oral piercings! It was the only thing that helped my tongue piercing heal. Two thumbs up
strengths: Clear, specific use cases that people actively look for relief from, like piercings and canker sores. This makes ad targeting and messaging easier.
Long history and a patented formula. Twenty years and a real story gives credibility that most new ecom brands do not have.
gaps: No reviews shown anywhere on the site. There is nothing for a cold shopper to trust, and social proof is what makes ads convert.
One flavor, Fresh Lemon Ice, is listed as out of stock and sold out. That cuts the sellable range in half right now.

game_plan.headline: Start with the mint mouthwash on one clear healing angle, prove it can sell cold, then widen and add the lemon flavor once it is back in stock.

phases.goal: Prove that a cold stranger will buy this mouthwash from an ad. Learn which angle and which audience works before spending real money.
campaigns: One testing campaign with 3 to 4 simple ad sets, each pointing at a different pain: oral piercing healing, canker sore relief, and post dental work recovery.
One retargeting campaign to catch people who clicked or viewed the product but did not buy.
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Arctic Ocean Mint. It is the only flavor in stock, mint is the safest broad appeal flavor for mouthwash, and its product page carries the fuller healing story. Lemon Ice is sold out so we cannot lead with it.
phases.creative: Run the ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet. Lead with the piercing-healing ad first, because the strongest real customer quote we have is about a tongue piercing healing, and that pain is urgent and specific. Second, run the canker sore and post dental angles. Keep creative plain and benefit-led: no burn, no alcohol, natural, safe for the whole family.
phases.what_success_looks_like: We find at least one angle and audience that brings in sales at a cost we are happy with. We are not chasing scale yet, we are finding the winner.

phases.goal: Put more money behind whatever won in month one and find more people like the buyers we already got.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad set and pause the losers.
Add lookalike audiences built from the buyers and site visitors we gathered in month one.
Expand retargeting with a small offer or bundle to push fence-sitters over the line.
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Arctic Ocean Mint, still the hero. Push the larger 16oz and 64oz sizes to lift the order value where it makes sense.
phases.creative: Make 2 to 3 new versions of the winning ad so it does not burn out. Keep the same winning angle but change the hook and the opening line.
phases.budget: Step up toward $250 to $300 a day only if month one hit its mark.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning campaign holds its return as we spend more, and lookalikes start bringing in new buyers steadily.

phases.goal: Scale the proven setup and add the second flavor if it is back in stock.
campaigns: Keep scaling the winning campaign in steady steps.
Launch Fresh Lemon Ice as a second product, if restocked, to buyers who like the mint and to fresh audiences.
Test one new pain angle we have not tried yet, like dry mouth or bad breath, to widen the top of the funnel.
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Arctic Ocean Mint as the anchor, plus Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Fresh Lemon Ice as the new flavor once stock is confirmed.
phases.creative: Fresh creative for the lemon flavor. Reuse the proven structure from the winning mint ads but swap in the zesty lemon angle.
phases.budget: Scale further based on results. No fixed number promised until we see months one and two.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two products selling from ads, a stable return we can grow, and a clear path to spend more each month.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: Enough to get roughly 30 to 40 clicks a day at typical costs in this category, which is the floor for learning which angle and audience works before we spend more.

estimates.label: Order value we planned around
estimates.basis: We took the median of their two listed products, both $11, and used it as a stand-in for average order value since we cannot see their real numbers. Note the mint page also shows a $17 price, so the true average may be higher once bigger sizes are counted.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We are not putting a 90 day revenue number on this yet
estimates.basis: At an $11 order value with no reviews and no ads running today, an honest revenue target needs their real order value across sizes and their margin first. We would set the target after the questions below.

assumptions: Fresh Lemon Ice comes back in stock before month three. If it stays out, phase 3 stays on mint only.
Their real average order value is higher than $11 because most people buy larger sizes or more than one bottle. If it truly sits at $11, the math for paid ads gets tight and we need bundles.
They can add real reviews or testimonials to the site soon. Ads to a page with zero social proof will convert worse.
Meta will approve the healing claims. Oral care with strong recovery language sometimes gets flagged, so copy must stay careful.

questions.question: Are you running any paid ads right now, on Meta or anywhere else?
questions.listening_for: We found no live Meta ads. Confirm if they never started, or stopped. If they stopped, why.

questions.question: Who handles marketing for the oral care line today, in-house or an agency?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is anyone to work with, and if a past agency left a bad taste.

questions.question: Have you ever run Meta ads before for this brand? What happened?
questions.listening_for: Past results, what broke, and whether expectations are realistic.

questions.question: What does a bottle actually cost you to make and ship, roughly? I want to make sure paid ads can pay for themselves.
questions.listening_for: Margin. At an $11 price, if margin is thin, ads may not work without bundles or bigger sizes.

questions.question: When someone buys, what do they usually grab, an 8oz or the bigger sizes, and do they come back and reorder?
questions.listening_for: Real order value and repeat buying. This changes the whole revenue math.

questions.question: I noticed there are no reviews on the site. Do you have reviews or testimonials somewhere we can pull onto the pages?
questions.listening_for: Whether social proof exists at all. This is the single biggest risk to ads converting. If they have reviews on Amazon or elsewhere, we can use them.

questions.question: The Fresh Lemon Ice flavor shows as sold out. When is it coming back?
questions.listening_for: Stock timing. It decides whether month three can add the second product.

questions.question: Which product and which use case sells best for you today, piercings, dental recovery, canker sores, or just daily use?
questions.listening_for: Confirms or corrects our guess to lead with the piercing angle. Their real bestseller should lead.

questions.question: When people land on your product page, do they buy, or do they drop off? Any sense of that?
questions.listening_for: Whether the problem is getting traffic or converting it. Changes if we fix ads first or the page first.

questions.question: Do you sell mostly on your own site, or is Amazon or retail a big part of it?
questions.listening_for: Where the money actually comes from. If Amazon dominates, the site may not be built to convert cold ad traffic yet.

questions.question: People clearly search for things like sea salt mouthwash and canker sore relief. Are you showing up when they do?
questions.listening_for: Honest opening for Google Search. These are real terms people type when they have the exact problem this product solves.

questions.question: You have a big Knowledge Center with articles and clinical studies. Is that bringing in any traffic from Google today?
questions.listening_for: Opening for SEO. They already have content, so the groundwork exists. If it gets no traffic, there is easy upside.

questions.question: Do you get much repeat business by email, or is that channel quiet right now?
questions.listening_for: Email is a cheap way to lift order value on an $11 product. If quiet, easy win to raise later.

objections.objection: We have been fine for 20 years without Meta ads, why start now?
objections.answer: That is exactly the point. You have a proven product and a real story, but strangers are not finding it on Meta at all. Right now that whole audience is going to competitors. We start small, prove one angle works, and only spend more once it does.

objections.objection: The mouthwash is only $11. There is no way ads make money on that.
objections.answer: You are right to worry about that, and it is the first thing we need to check together. Most people buy the bigger sizes or more than one bottle, and this is a product people reorder. If your real order value and margin support it, we build bundles to lift it. If they do not, I will tell you straight.

objections.objection: How do I know your ads will even work for us?
objections.answer: You do not yet, and I would not ask you to bet big on faith. Month one is only about proving it with a small daily budget. We find one thing that works before we scale. If nothing works, you have not risked much.

objections.objection: We do not have reviews on the site. Is that a problem?
objections.answer: It is the biggest thing we should fix, honestly. Cold shoppers trust other buyers more than they trust a brand. You clearly have happy customers, so let us get their words onto the pages before or right as we launch. It will lift every dollar we spend.

red_flags.flag: No reviews visible anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Ads send cold strangers to a page with nothing to trust. This lowers how many buy, no matter how good the ad is. We should fix this alongside launch.

red_flags.flag: Very low price at $11 a bottle.
red_flags.why_it_matters: At $11 an order, paid ads have little room to be profitable unless people buy bigger sizes, bundle, or reorder. We must confirm real order value and margin before promising revenue.

red_flags.flag: One of the two products, Fresh Lemon Ice, is sold out.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Half the catalog cannot be sold right now. It limits the plan to one product until it is restocked, and out of stock items hurt trust if people land on them.

red_flags.flag: Strong healing and recovery claims, including chemo mouth and infections.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Meta restricts health claims. Copy must avoid cure or treat language or ads get rejected. Our playbook already lists the phrases to avoid.

red_flags.flag: The email came from a gmail address while replies also came from the company domain.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Small mismatch worth noting. Confirm Eddie is the real decision maker who can approve budget, not just someone reviewing creative.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads on high-intent terms like sea salt mouthwash, canker sore relief, and mouthwash for piercings. People type these exact things when they have the problem this product solves.
SEO on their existing Knowledge Center. They already have articles and clinical studies built, so there may be easy traffic to win from Google without starting from scratch.
